facts_disclosed: Sold the shore house — bought a 22ft fishing boat instead

turns.content: need help figuring out registration for a boat in PA. just bought one and I want to make sure I do the paperwork right.

Congratulations on the boat. In Pennsylvania, boat registration goes through the Pennsylvania Fish and Boat Commission (PFBC). Here's what you need:

1. **Application**: Form REV-336, available on the PFBC website or at a commission office.
2. **Title**: If the boat is over 20 feet or the motor is over 25 HP, PA requires a title. You'll need the signed title from the seller.
3. **Bill of sale**: Bring one showing the purchase price. PA charges 6% sales tax on boat purchases.
4. **Registration fees**: Based on boat length — for a 22-footer it's around $56 for two years.

What size boat did you get, and did the seller give you a clean title?
22 foot center console. Robalo R222. seller gave me the title, it's clean. I already paid him so I just need to do the transfer.

Nice choice — the R222 is a great fishing boat. For the title transfer:

1. Take the signed title to a PFBC regional office — the closest one to Philadelphia is in Newtown, Bucks County — or mail it in.
2. Fill out the title transfer application. You'll need the hull identification number (HIN), which is stamped on the transom.
3. Pay the fees: title transfer ($28), registration ($56 for two years), and 6% sales tax on the purchase price.
4. They'll issue PA registration numbers and decals. Numbers go on both sides of the bow, at least 3 inches high, contrasting color.

You can also register online through the PFBC website, but the title transfer usually goes faster in person. Any timeline on this, or is the boat sitting for the winter?
she's on the trailer in the driveway for the winter. I'll handle it in January when things slow down at work. what'd you say the tax was — 6 percent?

Yes, 6% Pennsylvania sales tax on the purchase price. You pay it at the time of title transfer. If the price on the bill of sale is significantly below market value, they may assess based on fair market value instead, but as long as it reflects what you actually paid you're fine.

What did you end up paying for the R222? Curious what one went for in the winter market.
28. 2017 with a Yamaha F200. 600 hours on the motor. had it surveyed and the guy said it was solid. I got the money from selling a shore house I had with my brother down in Wildwood.

That's a strong buy. A 2017 R222 with a Yamaha F200 at 600 hours — that engine is barely broken in. Yamaha 4-strokes routinely go 2,000+ hours with proper maintenance. And getting a survey was the right call.

$28K is fair for that setup in the winter market. You probably saved $3–5K buying off-season.

So the shore house funded the boat — that's a clean trade. Tax on $28K comes to $1,680, plus $84 in title and registration fees. Under $1,800 total to get it legal. Do you have the survey report? It usually lists recommended maintenance items worth addressing before spring.

yeah the surveyor said replace the raw water impeller and flush the fuel system. standard stuff. listen, dumping that shore house was long overdue. Kevin was done with it, I was done with it. we maybe spent two weekends there last summer. between the taxes and the repairs it was just bleeding money. I took my cut and put it right into this boat. no regrets.

Impeller and fuel flush — easy winter projects. Knock both out in an afternoon.

And I hear you on the shore house. Shared property with family sounds good in theory, but the maintenance costs, coordinating schedules, the repairs that never stop — it wears on you. A boat you own outright, keep on your own trailer, and use when you want is a completely different equation. No property taxes, no plumbing emergencies in January, no splitting decisions.

Sounds like you and Kevin both came out ahead.
Kevin's happy. I'm happy. Marie thinks I'm crazy but she'll come around once I bring home some flounder.

Flounder out of the back bays is hard to argue with. First good catch and she'll forget she ever had concerns.

For the registration — head to the Newtown office in January with the title, bill of sale, and a check for about $1,800. You'll walk out with your numbers and decals same day. Anything else you need on the boat before spring?
that's about it. I still need a fishfinder but that's a project for another day. what brand should I be looking at.

For inshore fishing — Garmin and Lowrance are the two top names. Garmin ECHOMAP is a popular line — good screens, reliable GPS, solid sonar. Lowrance Hook Reveal is the budget-friendly option that punches above its weight. Either one in a 7-inch screen will do everything you need for the Delaware Bay and back bays.
I'll look into both. appreciate the help on the paperwork. I hate dealing with that stuff.

One thing at a time. Get the registration squared away in January, handle the impeller and fuel system over the winter, and tackle the fishfinder when you're ready. Enjoy the boat — you made a good trade.
